meta.title: American Independence & World Ideas
meta.subtitle: Choices, Symbols, and Shared Rights • Printable Web Lesson

hero.ariaLabel: American flag in a classroom for a history lesson about symbols and civic ideas
hero.imageQuery: American flag classroom liberty symbols history elementary hero

heroCopy.lede: History helps us understand how people made choices, worked for change, and used symbols to remember important civic ideas.
heroCopy.whatStudentsLearn: Students learn about colonies, the American Revolution, American symbols, and how ideas about fairness, rights, responsibility, and voice connect to people in many places.
learningTargets: I can explain what a colony was.
I can name one cause of the American Revolution.
I can explain how symbols tell stories about ideas.
I can connect fairness and voice to communities around the world.
studentDirections: Read the section and study the image before answering.
Use the vocabulary and quick facts to support your thinking.
Write in complete sentences unless the prompt says bullets are okay.
On paper: circle, label, or underline important evidence in the print image.

keyVocabulary.def: A settlement or place ruled by another country.
The ability of a country or group to govern itself.
An object, picture, or place that stands for an idea.
Freedoms or protections that people believe should be respected.

sections.imageQuery: colonial North America map historic settlements colonies
sections.title: Colonies in North America
sections.lede: Before the United States became a country, thirteen colonies in North America were ruled by Great Britain.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: A colony is ruled by another country.
Colonists lived far across the ocean from Britain.
People in the colonies had many different jobs and experiences.

cards.title: Map Mission
items: Find North America on a map.
Think about distance across the ocean.
Ask why distance could make governing hard.

prompt.label: Prompt A — Colony Clues
prompt.placeholder: Explain what a colony was and name one challenge colonists faced.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Colonies were settlements ruled by another country; challenges included distance, taxes, laws, and daily survival.

sections.ariaLabel: Historic map-style image for learning about colonies in North America

sections.imageQuery: American Revolution reenactment soldiers independence history
sections.title: The American Revolution
sections.lede: Many colonists wanted more say in the rules and taxes that affected them. This disagreement helped lead to the American Revolution.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: Some colonists thought taxes were unfair.
Many wanted representation, or a voice, in government.
The colonies declared independence in 1776.

cards.title: Cause and Effect
items: Cause: people disagreed about rules and taxes.
Effect: people organized, protested, and debated.
Effect: war and independence followed.

sections.callout: History often has more than one cause. Strong answers name a cause and explain the effect.

prompt.label: Prompt B — Cause and Effect
prompt.placeholder: Explain one reason some colonists wanted independence.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Students connect unfair taxes or lack of representation to the desire for independence.

sections.ariaLabel: Revolutionary War reenactors representing the American Revolution

sections.imageQuery: Liberty Bell American symbol independence liberty history
sections.title: Symbols Tell Stories
sections.lede: Flags, bells, documents, monuments, songs, and seals can remind people of important ideas and events.

cards.title: Vocabulary
items: Symbol: something that stands for an idea.
Liberty: freedom.
Independence: self-government.

cards.title: Look Closely
items: What symbol do you see?
What idea might it represent?
Who might understand it differently?

prompt.label: Prompt C — Symbol Meaning
prompt.placeholder: Choose one American symbol and explain what idea it represents.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Symbols can represent freedom, unity, independence, laws, or remembrance.

sections.ariaLabel: Liberty Bell as an American symbol connected to liberty and independence

sections.imageQuery: voting ballot civic voice rights fairness democracy
sections.title: Ideas Travel Around the World
sections.lede: Ideas about rights, fairness, responsibility, and self-government have been discussed by many people in many countries.

cards.title: World Connections
items: People in many places care about fairness.
Communities make rules to solve problems.
Having a voice means people can share ideas and concerns.

cards.title: Think Like a Historian
items: Ask who had a voice.
Ask who was left out.
Ask how ideas changed over time.

prompt.label: Prompt D — Shared Ideas
prompt.placeholder: Why might people in different places care about fairness and having a voice?
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Students identify fairness, rights, rules, voting, and community voice as shared civic ideas.

sections.ariaLabel: Ballot and voting scene representing civic voice and shared rights

sections.imageQuery: scales of justice law civic responsibility rights symbol
sections.title: Quick Check + Civic Poster
sections.lede: History helps us understand how people worked for change and how communities remember big ideas.

cards.title: Mini Quiz
items: What was a colony?
Name one reason for independence.
What is one civic idea people care about?

cards.title: Challenge
items: Choose one civic idea.
Make a poster with a clear symbol or slogan.
Explain how the idea helps a community.

promptE.label: Prompt E — Summary
promptE.placeholder: Summarize the colonies, independence, symbols, and shared ideas from this lesson.
promptE.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Summary includes American independence and connects it to broader ideas of rights and fairness.

promptF.label: Prompt F — Create
promptF.placeholder: Create a poster for one civic idea: fairness, freedom, responsibility, or having a voice.
promptF.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Poster names one idea, gives an example, and uses a clear symbol or slogan.

sections.ariaLabel: Scales of justice and law books for a civic ideas poster
